content.full_text:
California Fair Political Practices Commission

January 31, 1991

Roger Brown  
Law Offices  
11½ So. Washington St.  
Sonora, CA 95370

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance  
Our File No. I-90-747

Dear Mr. Brown:

We have received your letter of December 11, 1990, seeking written confirmation of the telephone advice I provided to you regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act.1 This is to confirm that your letter accurately reflects that advice.

Your letter does not identify on whose behalf you are seeking advice. Accordingly, we consider your request to be one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2

In the circumstances described in your letter, your client is an advertising agency which provides services to a political campaign committee. Per the agency’s contract with the committee, the agency receives commissions from the committee for all advertising created and placed for the committee.

In order to create and place the advertising, the agency client will subcontract with independent contractors for various services. It is the agency’s intention to share a portion of its commission from the campaign committee with those subcontractors. You stated in our telephone conversation of December 6, 1990, that the amount of the commissions is consistent with current industry practice. These commission payments are in addition to fees paid the independent contractors for the goods or services they provide

__________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

Roger Brown  
Page Two

and reported by the committee.

In this case, the advertising agency is not required to inform the campaign committee which subcontractors received a portion of the commission, nor how much each received. If making these commission payments is consistent with customary industry practice, and they are not made on behalf of the committee, they do not meet the definition of expenditures by an agent or independent contractor required to be reported by the campaign committee under Section 84303.

If you have further questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin  
Acting General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Braaten-Moen  
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure
